intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah is a local single-developer CLI that generates native configuration for four coding agents — Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI — from one Go source of truth, and ships a uniform protective toolset alongside every configuration: five event hooks (linter, command guard, secret scanner, comment/placeholder checker, session logger), a check-only built-in yaah MCP server, and a durable per-session audit log under .claude/sessions/. It performs no LLM inference of its own and makes no outbound calls beyond the configured provider, the opted-in MCP servers, and pinned package registries and git remotes. Its central declared promise is that these protections land uniformly on every generated target — catastrophic shell commands blocked before they execute, high-impact writes gated to a human checkpoint, and third-party MCP servers reached only over TLS and pinned to integrity-checked versions.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Go 1.25 cobra CLI (module dirien/yet-another-agent-harness) built on an Interface→Registry→Generator pattern. Blocking handlers live in pkg/hooks/handlers — command-guard on PreToolUse/Bash, secret-scanner and comment-checker on PostToolUse/Edit — and enforce by returning an exit-2 block through yaah hook; pkg/harness/harness.go records tool calls, blocked calls and file modifications to structured JSON under .claude/sessions/. Per-agent generators in pkg/generator emit each agent's native hook file, driven by a cross-agent event map in hookmap.go. The built-in MCP server (pkg/mcpserver) exposes seven check-only tools over stdio; .mcp.json wires context7 (npx -y), pulumi (HTTPS), and yaah itself. The Codex entry in hookmap.go leaves PreToolUse and PostToolUse blank, so codex.go emits neither the command-guard nor the secret-scanner hook for the Codex target.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is a single, well-scoped breach of the harness's central uniformity promise. The protective controls are genuinely operative: the command guard, secret scanner and comment checker each return a real exit-2 block on the Claude, OpenCode and Copilot targets, and a durable, structured per-session audit log is written under .claude/sessions/. But the Codex target ships without them, because hookmap.go leaves Codex's PreToolUse and PostToolUse events blank, so codex.go emits neither the command-guard nor the secret-scanner hook — a behavior codified, not caught, by TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported. The only protection reaching a Codex-configured agent is the check-only yaah MCP server, which is advisory and model-invoked, not an enforcing gate.

A secondary theme is supply-chain inconsistency: remote skills and agents are pinned to full commit SHAs and Go dependencies carry a committed go.sum, yet the third-party MCP servers are fetched unpinned via npx -y and reinstalled fresh on every run. The remaining findings are narrow-scope gaps around the same operative controls — a denylist that misses long-form flag variants, an ungated planning-init writer, an opt-in provider that embeds a token into generated config, and the absence of any adversarial test or threat model to confirm the protections hold.

rules.rule_text: Weakening or removing the protective hooks from a generated configuration so that an agent ships without the full protection set.
Fetching yaah itself and third-party MCP server packages; MUST resolve pinned, integrity-checked versions.
Hardcoded credentials MUST be detected before an edit that introduces them is accepted; they MUST NOT be written into committed files unremarked.
Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets content the agent ingested from an untrusted source persist into future sessions unreviewed.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Wiring in any third-party MCP server, remote skill/agent, or marketplace plugin beyond the defaults.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Every generated configuration MUST carry the complete set, not a subset.

MCP tool descriptions — the built-in server's, and, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures — MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model (tool-poisoning indicator).
Reaching a remote MCP server over a non-TLS connection.
A `yaah generate` run emits the target agent's config files plus `.mcp.json`, all carrying the same protective hook set.
The project maintains a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.

The project publishes a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and its own protections are adversarially tested — confirming the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

findings.summary: The generated Codex configuration omits the command guard, secret scanner and comment checker — the hook event map leaves Codex's PreToolUse and PostToolUse blank.
findings.description: In hookmap.go the Codex column is empty for PreToolUse and PostToolUse, so codex.go emits no command-guard or secret-scanner hook for the Codex target — a behavior codified by TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported. Catastrophic shell commands and credential-bearing edits therefore reach a Codex-configured agent without yaah's enforcing block, and per-tool session audit is not captured. The remit's escape hatch (deliver equivalents via MCP) is only partly met: the check-only yaah MCP server is wired in but is advisory and model-invoked, not a gate.
policy_rule_text: Weakening or removing the protective hooks from a generated configuration so that an agent ships without the full protection set.
Hardcoded credentials MUST be detected before an edit that introduces them is accepted; they MUST NOT be written into committed files unremarked.
Every generated configuration MUST carry the complete set, not a subset.
A `yaah generate` run emits the target agent's config files plus `.mcp.json`, all carrying the same protective hook set.

evidence.snippet: HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse mappings (lines 30-39) set Claude/OpenCode/Copilot event names but leave Codex blank, so CodexEventName returns "" for both — the two events that carry the command guard and secret scanner.
GenerateHooks skips any event where CodexEventName=="" (lines 88-93); with Pre/PostToolUse blank the Codex hooks.json carries only SessionStart and Stop — no command-guard or secret-scanner hook.

recommended_actions: Deliver an enforcing equivalent for Codex: map Pre/PostToolUse to Codex's real hook surface if it exists, or gate the Codex agent's shell/edit path through a blocking wrapper rather than the advisory-only MCP tools.
Replace the TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported assertion with a test that fails unless every generated target carries the full protective set (or a documented enforcing equivalent).

findings.summary: Third-party MCP servers are fetched unpinned and reinstalled fresh on every run via npx -y, violating the harness's own MCP pinning requirement.
findings.description: The context7 provider launches npx -y @context7/mcp with no version pin, resolving latest on every run; the opt-in Notion provider does the same. This is the exact rug-pull / dependency-confusion surface the remit forbids, and it is inconsistent with the codebase's otherwise-strong pinning — remote skills and agents use full commit SHAs and Go deps carry a committed go.sum.
policy_rule_text: Fetching yaah itself and third-party MCP server packages; MUST resolve pinned, integrity-checked versions.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: Server() returns Command "npx", Args ["-y", "@context7/mcp"] — no version pin, `-y` auto-confirms install, latest resolved on every launch.
Generated context7 entry ships the same unpinned `npx -y @context7/mcp` into the project MCP config that every configured agent loads.

recommended_actions: Pin third-party MCP server packages to an exact version (e.g. @context7/mcp@<version>) and prefer an integrity-checked install, matching the SHA-pinning already used for remote skills.
Apply the same pinning to the Notion provider's npx -y invocation.

findings.summary: The command guard is a 7-pattern regex denylist that catches canonical forms but misses long-form and reordered flag variants of the same catastrophic commands.
findings.description: The guard blocks rm -rf / but not rm --recursive --force /, rm -fr /, or rm -r -f /; it blocks git push --force to main but not git push -f or --force-with-lease. Because it matches regexes against a Turing-complete shell string, equivalent invocations pass. The remit calls out exactly this coverage question as a risk sensitivity.
policy_rule_text: Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.

evidence.snippet: Default blocked patterns (lines 29-36) hardcode `rm\s+-rf\s+/` and `git\s+push\s+--force\s+(origin\s+)?(main|master)` — short-form/flag-order and long-form variants (`--recursive --force`, `-fr`, `-f`) are not covered.

recommended_actions: Normalize the command (expand short/long flags, canonicalize order) before matching, or match on parsed argv rather than a raw regex; add the long-form and reordered variants to the denylist as an interim step.

findings.summary: Session-loaded config files that yaah generates (AGENTS.md, skills, agents) are writable with no write-gate against poisoning — the only Edit-time hooks scan for secrets and placeholder comments.
findings.description: The remit names writability of generated skills/agents/AGENTS.md as a memory-poisoning persistence surface, but yaah provides no PreToolUse path-guard on writes to these files — the command guard is Bash-only, and the PostToolUse handlers check for secrets and comments, not for content the agent ingested from an untrusted source re-entering context. Writes are recorded to the session log, but not gated or reviewed.
policy_rule_text: Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets content the agent ingested from an untrusted source persist into future sessions unreviewed.

evidence.snippet: CommandGuard.Events() is PreToolUse and Match() is Bash-only (lines 49-57) — there is no PreToolUse gate on Edit/Write to session-loaded config paths.
Edit/Write to any path (lines 144-150) is appended to FilesModified and logged, but never blocked or diffed for review — including writes to AGENTS.md and generated skills/agents.

recommended_actions: Add a PreToolUse gate that flags or blocks agent writes to session-loaded config files (AGENTS.md, generated skills/agents) so poisoning of the runtime context surface requires review.

findings.summary: The yaah_planning_init MCP tool writes scaffolding into the workspace with no server-side approval gate, though the remit classifies it as approval-required.
findings.description: The remit lists yaah_planning_init as the one built-in write tool requiring the developer's review, but the MCP server registers it as an ordinary callable tool with no annotation or gate. Blast radius is constrained — it creates a fixed .planning/ directory tree and STATE.md and refuses if the directory already exists — and MCP tool approval is ultimately the host agent's responsibility, which is why this is Medium rather than a Critical approval-gap.
policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

evidence.snippet: addPlanningInitTool (lines 363-413) registers the write tool and calls os.MkdirAll/os.WriteFile directly, with no approval hook, disable-model-invocation flag, or confirmation.
registerTools() adds yaah_planning_init alongside the six check-only tools with no differentiation for its write capability.

recommended_actions: Mark yaah_planning_init as requiring confirmation (e.g. surface it as a restricted/disable-model-invocation tool) so the developer reviews the workspace write, rather than relying solely on the host agent's gating.

findings.summary: The Notion MCP provider interpolates a bearer token literal into the generated config env, writing credential material into files that are typically committed.
findings.description: When a developer opts into Notion, the provider embeds the supplied token directly into the OPENAPI_MCP_HEADERS env value as a Bearer literal, which the generators then serialize into .mcp.json / .copilot/mcp-config.json in cleartext. No remit rule maps cleanly (this is a credential-hygiene pattern, not a divergence from a specific clause), but it turns an opt-in integration into a plaintext-secret-in-config exposure; it is Medium because it is off by default and uses the developer's own token.

evidence.snippet: Server() builds Env OPENAPI_MCP_HEADERS as a JSON string containing `Bearer ` + n.apiToken (lines 27-29) — the literal token is embedded into the generated MCP config env.

recommended_actions: Reference the token from an environment variable at runtime (e.g. emit ${NOTION_TOKEN}) instead of interpolating its literal value into the generated config files.

findings.summary: No published threat model or security-disclosure process and no adversarial test — the suite is functional-only, and one test codifies the Codex protection gap rather than flagging it.
findings.description: The remit expects a published threat model, a disclosure process, and adversarial tests confirming the guard blocks a catastrophic command, the scanner catches a planted secret, poisoning is prevented, and each generated config carries its protections. The repo has none of these at root, and TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported asserts that Codex ships without the protective hooks — the opposite of a uniformity check.
policy_rule_text: The project publishes a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and its own protections are adversarially tested — confirming the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

evidence.snippet: TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported (lines 118-136) asserts PreToolUse produces nil hooks for Codex ("PreToolUse is not supported by Codex") — encoding the protection gap as expected rather than testing that protections are delivered.

No SECURITY.md, threat model, or disclosure process exists at repo root (confirmed by workspace sweep); AGENTS.md's "Never Do" note that hooks "are security controls" is the only security-posture statement.

recommended_actions: Add a SECURITY.md with a disclosure process and a threat model document covering the command guard, secret scanner, and session-loaded-file poisoning surface.
Add an adversarial test that plants a catastrophic command and a secret against each generated target and asserts they are blocked, so the uniformity promise is enforced by CI.

positives.title: Deterministic command-guard block on shell activity
positives.description: The command guard runs as a PreToolUse/Bash handler and returns a blocking exit-2 result through yaah hook, so catastrophic patterns are stopped before execution on the Claude, OpenCode and Copilot targets.

positives.title: Secret scanner blocks credential-bearing edits
positives.description: A PostToolUse/Edit handler with 13 default credential patterns returns Block:true on a match, refusing the edit and reporting location and pattern without transmitting the value.

positives.title: Durable structured per-session audit log
positives.description: HandleHookEvent records tool calls, blocked calls and file modifications to atomic-written JSON under .claude/sessions/<id>.json, reconstructable via yaah session show.

positives.title: Remote skills and agents pinned to commit SHAs
positives.description: Every remote skill and agent is registered against a full 40-char commit SHA rather than a mutable branch or tag, closing the rug-pull surface for remote content.

positives.title: Go dependencies pinned with committed lockfile
positives.description: The dependency tree is small (two direct requires) and pinned to exact versions with a committed go.sum, satisfying the supply-chain lockfile baseline.

positives.title: Remote MCP over TLS and non-instructional tool descriptions
positives.description: The pulumi MCP server is reached over HTTPS, and the built-in server's seven tool descriptions are plain check-only text with no instruction-like language directed at the model (no tool-poisoning indicator).

positives.title: Session ID sanitization prevents path traversal
positives.description: The session store rejects IDs containing path separators or relative components before using them as filenames, preventing traversal into arbitrary paths.

rows.purpose: Tool calls, blocked calls, files modified, event counts per Claude Code session
Session lifecycle events (SessionStart/SessionEnd/Stop) only — no tool-call detail

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial posture overall. yaah pairs genuinely operative Zero-Trust interposition — a blocking command guard, secret scanner and comment checker — and a durable structured session audit log with strong-but-uneven supply-chain hygiene, then gives ground on a Red-Team practice that is essentially functional-tests-only and on a uniformity gap that leaves one of its four generated targets, Codex, without the enforcing controls the harness promises. The controls that exist are real and they enforce; the shortfall is coverage and adversarial validation, not security theater.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: yaah's own capability surface is tightly scoped to config generation, local static checks and check-only MCP tools — the CLI exposes a fixed command set and the built-in MCP tools are read/check-only except one constrained writer, with no general-purpose or inference surface; the dozens of remote skills/agents enabled by AllDefaults are the configured downstream agent's capabilities, not yaah's.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: yaah performs no inference and its secret scanner actively keeps credentials out of committed edits, but the session-loaded config files it generates (AGENTS.md, skills, agents) are writable with no poisoning write-gate (PRAX-2026-07-17-004) and the opt-in Notion provider interpolates a bearer token into generated config (PRAX-2026-07-17-006).

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real deterministic interposition exists on the agent's tool path — the command guard, secret scanner and comment checker each return a blocking exit-2 result through yaah hook on Claude, OpenCode and Copilot — but that enforcement is absent for the Codex target (PRAX-2026-07-17-001), the command-guard denylist is narrow (PRAX-2026-07-17-003), and the yaah_planning_init writer is ungated server-side (PRAX-2026-07-17-005).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Go dependencies are pinned with a committed go.sum, remote skills and agents are pinned to full commit SHAs, and CI pins action SHAs with cosign and SBOM per AGENTS.md — strong provenance overall — but the third-party MCP servers are fetched unpinned via npx -y and reinstalled fresh on every run (PRAX-2026-07-17-002).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Functional unit tests cover handlers and generators, but there is no adversarial testing, no published threat model or disclosure process, and no test asserting the protective set is delivered uniformly — indeed TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported codifies the Codex protection gap rather than flagging it (PRAX-2026-07-17-007).

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The harness writes a durable, structured, timestamped per-session audit log to .claude/sessions/<id>.json capturing tool calls, blocked calls and file modifications, queryable via yaah session show/list; the gaps are that the Session.Findings array is never populated and the parallel plaintext .claude/logs/sessions.log records only session lifecycle.
